claim: Between March and June 2020, the Federal Reserve expanded its balance sheet by approximately $3 trillion in response to the COVID-19 financial-market disruption. The S&P 500 fell 34% from its February 19, 2020 peak (3,386) to its March 23 trough (2,237) and recovered the prior peak by August 18, 2020. U.S. billionaire wealth increased by approximately $1.3 trillion between March and September 2020, per the Institute for Policy Studies. U.S. real average hourly earnings then declined for 25 consecutive months from April 2021 through April 2023 as post-stimulus inflation outran wages.

sources.label: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System — H.4.1 Statistical Release (Factors Affecting Reserve Balances), weekly balance-sheet data, March–June 2020.
Institute for Policy Studies, “Billionaire Bonanza 2020: Wealth Windfalls, Tumbling Taxes, and Pandemic Profiteers” (Collins, Ocampo, Paslaski; April 2020 and follow-on updates through 2021).
S&P Dow Jones Indices — S&P 500 historical daily close data (Feb 19, 2020 peak at 3,386.15; Mar 23, 2020 trough at 2,237.40; Aug 18, 2020 prior-peak recovery).
U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics — Real Earnings news releases, April 2021 through April 2023 (25 consecutive months of year-over-year decline in real average hourly earnings).

notes: Fed H.4.1 is the canonical primary for the balance-sheet expansion (from ~$4.2T in early March 2020 to ~$7.2T by mid-June 2020 — roughly $3T). The IPS billionaire-wealth series is the most-cited secondary tracking; their methodology draws on Forbes real-time data, which has known limitations but is the public-facing standard. The S&P recovery timeline (Feb 19 → Mar 23 → Aug 18) is verifiable across multiple market-data providers. BLS's 25-month consecutive real-earnings decline is a well-publicized statistic during the post-pandemic inflation cycle.
